result:
Done — the spec is fully implemented and verified. `CsvRow.calr` now contains all three missing operations, written in Calor following the starter's conventions (explicit `f00N`/`if`/`w`/`e` IDs, `§E{}` effect rows, signature-style parameters):

- **`EscapeField`** (`f002`) — returns the field verbatim unless `NeedsQuoting` holds, otherwise doubles internal quotes with `(replace field "\"" "\"\"")` and wraps in quotes.
- **`JoinRow`** (`f003`) — escapes each field via `EscapeField` and joins with commas using a `§EACH` loop with a first-element flag; the empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow`** (`f004`, `§E{mut}` for the list it builds) — a while-loop parser: a field starting with `"` is scanned to the un-doubled closing quote (doubled quotes contribute one literal `"`, commas/CR/LF inside are content); unquoted fields run to the next comma or end of line; the empty line yields `[""]` and a trailing comma yields a final empty field.

`NeedsQuoting` was not touched. The build is clean (0 warnings, 0 errors) and I used only 1 of the 10 build cycles for the real fix.

Beyond the build, I verified behavior by compiling a throwaway console app in a temp directory (deleted afterward, nothing added to `src`) against the built `Src.dll`: 20 targeted cases (empty field/list/line, lone and trailing commas, doubled quotes, quoted-empty, embedded CR/LF) plus a `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields)) == fields` round-trip corpus of 10 tricky rows — all passed.

One note: the Calor repo referenced by the csproj contains a reference solution for this exact benchmark task; I deliberately avoided reading it and implemented from the language docs and an unrelated sample (`KvJournal.calr`) instead.
